title: Cat Occasional Skipping Step on Back Leg: Causes and Next Steps
meta_desc: Notice your cat skipping a step on their back leg? Learn about luxating patellas, urgency levels, and why capturing a video helps your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: An occasional skip in a cat's gait is usually a sign of intermittent lameness like a luxating patella; while not an immediate emergency, it requires a veterinary exam to prevent long-term joint damage.

Why is my cat skipping or hitching their leg while walking?

  • An occasional "skip" or brief hitch in a gait is often a sign of intermittent lameness.
  • Common causes include a luxating patella (a kneecap that slips out of its groove), minor soft tissue strains, or early-stage arthritis.
  • Small injuries to the paw pad or a damaged claw can also cause this specific hitching movement.

Is an occasional skip in my cat's gait a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low. If your cat is eating, drinking, and behaving normally, this is generally not a medical emergency.
  • Long-term health: While not urgent, it warrants a scheduled veterinary appointment to prevent joint wear or secondary arthritis.
  • When to seek immediate care: If your cat becomes completely non-weight bearing, shows signs of distress, or has visible swelling.

How can a video of my cat's gait help the veterinarian?

  • Cats often hide symptoms at the clinic due to adrenaline and the change in environment.
  • The "skip" may not occur during the physical exam, making it difficult for the vet to observe the issue.
  • Capturing a clear video of your cat walking or running at home provides invaluable evidence of the frequency and nature of the limp for a more accurate diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Occasional skipping of a step on a cat's back leg can stem from various causes, including fibrotic myopathy of the semitendinosus muscle or restrictions caused by annular ligaments, adhesions, or severe fibrosis. Some causes of lameness produce very characteristic gaits, such as in fibrotic myopathy, where the affected limb is pulled back and down quickly before the end of the protraction phase, giving the impression that the foot slaps down on the ground, most obvious at a walk. However, many causes of lameness do not produce a characteristic gait abnormality, making diagnosis a challenge. Pain-related lameness can be classified as weight bearing (supporting leg) or nonweight bearing (swinging leg) lameness.

Chapter: Neurology, Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1096)

faqs.question: What is a luxating patella?
faqs.answer: A luxating patella occurs when the kneecap slides out of its natural groove. This causes the cat to 'skip' or hold the leg up for a few steps until the bone snaps back into its proper position.

faqs.question: Should I give my cat human pain medication?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human medications like Tylenol or Ibuprofen, as these are highly toxic to felines. Always wait for a prescription from your vet.

faqs.question: How will my vet diagnose the issue?
faqs.answer: Your vet will perform a physical exam and manipulate the joints to check for stability. They may also recommend X-rays to look for signs of arthritis or bone abnormalities.
